questions.explanation: A variable is a named place in memory (conceptually) used to store data that may change during program execution.

options.A: A named storage location for a value that can change
options.B: A fixed hardware register inside the CPU
options.C: A network endpoint used to fetch data

questions.question: In programming, what does a variable primarily represent?

questions.explanation: A bit is the fundamental unit of digital information and can hold one of two values: 0 or 1.

options.B: The smallest unit of data, either 0 or 1

questions.question: In digital systems, what is a bit?

questions.explanation: In Kotlin, 'val' declares an immutable reference (read-only), while 'var' is mutable.
questions.question: Which Kotlin keyword declares a read-only reference (cannot be reassigned)?

questions.explanation: An IDE integrates editor, build tools, debugging, and project navigation to streamline development.

options.B: To provide tools like code editing, building, debugging, and project management in one place
options.C: To host REST APIs for mobile apps
options.D: To encrypt application data at rest

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of an Integrated Development Environment (IDE) for app development?

questions.explanation: Module-level build.gradle(.kts) specifies dependencies, compileSdk, defaultConfig, buildTypes, and other module build settings.
questions.question: In Android Studio, which file typically defines app-level dependencies and build configuration for a module?

questions.explanation: A ViewModel is designed to store and manage UI-related data in a lifecycle-conscious way and survive configuration changes.
questions.question: In MVVM on Android, which component should expose UI state and handle UI-related logic while surviving configuration changes?

questions.explanation: @Composable identifies functions that describe UI and can be re-invoked by the Compose runtime during recomposition.

options.A: It marks a function as safe to run on the main thread only
options.B: It marks a function that can emit UI and participate in Compose's recomposition
options.C: It generates a Room database schema at compile time
options.D: It enables ProGuard/R8 rules for the function

questions.question: In Jetpack Compose, what is the main purpose of the @Composable annotation?

questions.explanation: Suspend-based Retrofit calls executed in coroutines keep the UI responsive and use JSON converters for reliable parsing.

options.A: Run the network call on the main thread and parse JSON in the Activity
options.B: Use Kotlin coroutines with Retrofit (suspend functions) and parse JSON via a converter (e.g., Moshi/Gson)
options.C: Use Thread.sleep() to wait for the response, then parse JSON
options.D: Store the URL in SharedPreferences and let the system fetch it automatically

questions.question: You need to call a REST API and parse JSON without blocking the UI. Which approach best matches modern Android practices?

questions.explanation: collectAsStateWithLifecycle bridges Flow to Compose State in a lifecycle-aware way, triggering recomposition when data changes.

options.A: Call flow.collect { ... } directly inside the Composable body
options.B: Convert Flow to state using collectAsStateWithLifecycle (or collectAsState in simpler cases) and read the state in UI
options.C: Use runBlocking in the Composable to get the list synchronously
options.D: Read the database on the main thread and manually call invalidate()

questions.question: A Room DAO returns a Flow<List<User>>. In a Compose screen, what is the recommended way to observe it so the UI updates automatically and respects lifecycle?

questions.explanation: A Retrofit client is typically an app-wide dependency; providing it in a Hilt module installed in SingletonComponent with @Singleton ensures one shared instance.

options.A: In an Activity with no scope, so each screen gets a fresh instance
options.B: In a @HiltViewModel with @ViewModelScoped, so it survives process death
options.C: In a @Module installed in SingletonComponent with @Singleton scope
options.D: In AndroidManifest.xml as a <service> so Hilt can discover it

questions.question: In a multi-module Android app using Hilt, you want to provide a single Retrofit instance shared across the app. Where should you define the provider and which scope is most appropriate?
